steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The study found that participants recruited via snowball sampling (i.e., through their own social networks) had a much higher retention rate than those recruited by strangers. The blank must explain why that difference exists — what does being recruited by someone you know do that a stranger cannot?

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘being recruited to participate in a study by someone with whom one is socially connected may impart a feeling of obligation to persist with participation in the study.’: A social connection creates accountability — you don't want to let down someone you know — which explains why retention is higher among snowball-sampled participants. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘people with relatively small social networks are inherently less likely to be recruited to participate in a study via snowball sampling than are people with relatively large social networks.’: This is about who gets recruited, not about why recruits stay once enrolled. It does not address retention. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘snowball sampling is more likely to improve retention rates among rural participants than among nonrural participants.’: The study compared two rural counties only; no urban or nonrural group was studied, so this rural/nonrural comparison is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘snowball sampling is more effective at producing nationally representative samples than other recruitment methods are.’: Representativeness was never measured or mentioned in the passage. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
